frameworks.name: categorical perception
frameworks.one_line: perceptual system commits to one interpretation, discarding rival hypotheses' probabilities

frameworks.name: normative decision theory
frameworks.one_line: optimal action weighting requires probabilities over all possible world states

core_question: Does categorical perception cause humans to deviate from normative integration of perceptual and action uncertainty?
key_assumptions: optimal choice requires utility weighted by full posterior over states
categorization collapses the posterior to a single interpretation
perceptual and motor uncertainty can be jointly manipulated in a pointing task
manipulations: modality of perceptual evidence (visual vs auditory)
perceptual uncertainty vs action/motor uncertainty trade-off
reward contingencies tied to combined uncertainties
key_findings: observers overweighted perceptual uncertainty relative to motor uncertainty
the suboptimality held across both visual and auditory tasks
deviations are quantitatively explained by a categorical-perception model
interpretations: categorization discards probability information needed for normative action selection
perceptual architecture imposes a structural limit on normative decision making
